title: Unit 26 Hyper-Exhaustive Masterclass: Active & Passive Participles (Οι Μετοχές)

script.text: Welcome... to the Hyper-Exhaustive Grammar Masterclass for Unit 35: Participles. These are the 'Verbal Adjectives'. Words like 'running', 'broken', or 'tired'. In Greek, they come in two flavors: Active and Passive. I'm Ava, and I'm joined by Athina. Athina... why are active participles so 'stable'?
Γεια σας! The Active Participles are wonderful because they are indeclinable. They never change their ending! They always end in '-on-tas' or '-ōn-tas'.
Indeclinable... that’s music to my ears. So if I want to say 'running', I take the verb 'Tré-khō' and make it 'Tré-khon-tas'?
Exactly. 'Eer-the tré-khon-tas'... he came running. 'Ír-than tré-khon-tas'... they came running. The word 'Tré-khon-tas' stays exactly the same, whether it's one person or many.
That’s perfect for describing 'how' an action is done. But what about the other kind? The Passive Participles. These are the ones that describe a state, like 'tired'.

Ah, these are more 'traditional'. They end in '-mé-nos'. 'Kou-ra-smé-nos' (tired), 'Ghra-mmé-nos' (written), 'A-noigh-mé-nos' (opened). And because they act like adjectives, they MUST match the noun in gender and case.
So 'Ee pór-ta ee-nai a-noigh-mé-nee'... the door is opened. 'To spée-ti ee-nai ghra-mmé-no'... the house is written (maybe in a deed?). They rhyme with their subjects.
Correct. We use these all the time as adjectives. 'En-a kou-ra-smé-no pai-dée'... a tired child. They behave exactly like the 'Ka-lós' group we learned way back in Unit 6.

Pro Tip: If you want to describe 'while doing something', use '-on-tas'. If you want to describe the 'result' of something, use '-mé-nos'. Athina, what’s the difference between '-on-tas' with an 'o' and '-on-tas' with an 'ōmega'?
It’s a spelling rule! If the stress is on the 'o', we use omikron: 'Tré-khon-tas'. If the stress is earlier, we use ōmega: 'Ghrá-phon-tas' (writing). But the sound is the same!
It’s all about where the beat falls. This concludes Unit 35. You now know how to add movement and state to your Greek. Excellent work.
Μπράβο! Τα λέμε στο επόμενο μάθημα. Γεια σας!
Excellent work. We’ll see you in Unit 36.
